tipsAndTricks: Add CLAUDE.md to your repo root — CC reads it automatically and it becomes your persistent project context
Use --print mode for shell pipelines: claude --print "summarise this" < file.txt
--permission-mode bypassPermissions for trusted repos eliminates the approval friction completely
Pipe stdin: cat error.log | claude "why is this failing" — CC reads stdin natively
The Task tool lets CC spawn parallel sub-agents — mention it in your prompt for large refactors
--continue resumes your last session without re-explaining context
--output-format json is your friend for scripting CC into CI pipelines

quickStart.title: Get productive in 30 seconds
steps: From your repo root, run `claude` for interactive work or `claude -p "your task"` for one-shot/headless work.
For anything non-trivial, start with a verification target: tell Claude what command proves success, like `npm test`, `pnpm lint`, or a screenshot comparison.
Use `@path/to/file` to point Claude at the exact file instead of describing it vaguely.
For bigger changes, enter Plan Mode first with `/plan` or `--permission-mode plan` so Claude explores before editing.
After changes, ask Claude to run the relevant tests, summarize risks, and create the commit or PR if you want the full workflow handled.

dailyWorkflows.title: Fix a bug from an error message
dailyWorkflows.description: Paste the real error, give the reproduce command, and make Claude verify the fix instead of guessing.
dailyWorkflows.command: claude "The app fails when I run npm test with this error: [paste stack trace]. Reproduce it, find the root cause, fix it, and verify the failing test passes."

dailyWorkflows.title: Understand an unfamiliar codebase fast
dailyWorkflows.description: Have Claude map the architecture, key flows, and hotspots before you touch code.
dailyWorkflows.command: claude --permission-mode plan "Give me a high-level overview of this codebase, identify the main architecture patterns, trace the auth flow, and list the files I should read first."

dailyWorkflows.title: Targeted code explanation with exact files
dailyWorkflows.description: Use `@` references so Claude starts with the right context instead of scanning the whole repo.
dailyWorkflows.command: claude "Explain how @src/auth/session.ts and @src/api/login.ts work together, and point out likely failure points."

dailyWorkflows.title: Write tests for an existing module
dailyWorkflows.description: Ask for missing coverage plus edge cases, then have Claude run and fix the new tests.
dailyWorkflows.command: claude "Find gaps in test coverage for @src/services/notifications.ts, add meaningful tests for happy path and edge cases, run the relevant test file, and fix any failures."

dailyWorkflows.title: Safe refactor in stages
dailyWorkflows.description: Use plan mode first, then implement against the plan so the change stays scoped.
dailyWorkflows.command: claude --permission-mode plan "Analyze @src/utils and create a safe refactor plan to modernize deprecated API usage without changing behavior."

dailyWorkflows.title: Continue yesterday's work
dailyWorkflows.description: Resume the last conversation or a named session instead of restating everything.

dailyWorkflows.title: Review PR comments and fix issues
dailyWorkflows.description: Pull PR review comments directly into the session, then have Claude address them.
dailyWorkflows.command:
claude
/pr-comments 123
"Address all unresolved review comments, run tests, and commit the fixes."

dailyWorkflows.title: Ask a side question without polluting the thread
dailyWorkflows.description: Use /btw to get a quick answer mid-session without it becoming part of the conversation history.
dailyWorkflows.command: /btw What's the difference between useEffect and useLayoutEffect here?

dailyWorkflows.title: Use Claude like a Unix tool
dailyWorkflows.description: Pipe logs, diffs, or command output straight into headless mode for quick analysis.
dailyWorkflows.command: git diff main --name-only | claude -p "Review these changed files for security issues and list the top risks first."

powerMoves.title: Explore -> Plan -> Implement -> Verify
powerMoves.description: Power users split discovery from coding. First use Plan Mode to understand the codebase and produce a plan, then switch to implementation with explicit verification steps.
powerMoves.command: claude --permission-mode plan "Read @src/auth and plan an OAuth migration. Identify files, risks, tests, and rollback concerns."
powerMoves.why: This avoids the biggest beginner mistake: letting Claude start coding before it fully understands the problem.

powerMoves.title: Make Claude self-check every change
powerMoves.description: Always specify how Claude should prove success: a test command, linter, screenshot, or exact expected output.
powerMoves.command: claude "Add retry logic to the payment client, run pnpm test payment-client, and only stop when the tests pass and there are no new lint errors."
powerMoves.why: Verification is the highest-leverage prompt upgrade because it closes the loop without needing you to babysit every step.

powerMoves.title: Use `@files` to slash token waste
powerMoves.description: Instead of saying 'look around the repo', reference the exact files or directory that matter.
powerMoves.command: claude "Compare @src/db/schema.ts with @src/db/migrations and tell me why signup is failing in production."
powerMoves.why: Precise file references reduce context bloat and get better answers faster.

powerMoves.title: Create a project CLAUDE.md that actually helps
powerMoves.description: Run `/init` (or set CLAUDE_CODE_NEW_INIT=true for an interactive setup that also covers skills, hooks, and personal memory). Keep the file short: build commands, test rules, repo conventions, dangerous areas.
powerMoves.why: A concise CLAUDE.md compounds over time; a bloated one gets ignored and pollutes every session.

powerMoves.title: Use subagents for specialist work
powerMoves.description: Create a debugger, reviewer, or API designer subagent so research and implementation happen in separate context windows. Use `isolation: "worktree"` to give each subagent its own git worktree.
powerMoves.why: Subagents preserve main-session context, reduce noise, and can be constrained to the exact tools/models needed.

powerMoves.title: Use hooks for rules that must happen every time
powerMoves.description: Wire up shell commands, HTTP endpoints, or LLM prompts to hook events. Key events: PreToolUse (block/modify before a tool runs), PostToolUse (run linters/tests after edits), SubagentStart/Stop (scope or clean up subagents), SessionStart, Stop, PreCompact/PostCompact.
powerMoves.why: CLAUDE.md is advisory; hooks are deterministic. That's the difference between 'usually' and 'always'.

powerMoves.title: Use worktrees for isolated feature work
powerMoves.description: Start Claude in an isolated git worktree so main-branch state is never touched during experiments.
powerMoves.command: claude --worktree "Implement rate limiter on a clean branch without touching main"
powerMoves.why: Worktrees let you run parallel Claude sessions on the same repo without conflicts. Each subagent can also use worktree isolation.

powerMoves.title: Compact before the session gets dumb
powerMoves.description: When context starts getting heavy, compact with an explicit focus so Claude keeps the right thread.
powerMoves.command: /compact Keep the current debugging state, reproduction steps, and the hypothesis about token refresh failures.
powerMoves.why: Claude quality drops as context fills. Power users actively manage context instead of hoping it stays sharp forever.

powerMoves.title: Use headless mode in scripts and CI
powerMoves.description: For repeatable automation, call Claude with `-p`, structured output, turn limits, and a budget ceiling. Use `--bare` to skip hook/plugin/skill discovery for faster scripted calls.
powerMoves.command: claude --bare -p --output-format json --max-turns 3 --max-budget-usd 2.00 "Review this diff for security regressions and output JSON with severity, file, and recommendation."
powerMoves.why: This makes Claude reliable in automation instead of interactive-only. --bare removes startup overhead that only matters for interactive sessions.

powerMoves.title: Enable remote control to pick up from browser or mobile
powerMoves.description: Make your local terminal session available for remote control from claude.ai or the Claude app. Or start a standalone remote-control server without a local session.
powerMoves.command:
/remote-control
# or from CLI without a local session:
claude remote-control --name "My Project"
powerMoves.why: You don't have to keep a laptop session open for long autonomous tasks. Continue from any device.

powerMoves.title: Raise effort only when the problem deserves it
powerMoves.description: Keep everyday coding on default. Use `/effort high` for architecture or stubborn bugs. `/effort max` is session-only and requires Opus 4.6.
powerMoves.why: This matches compute to task complexity instead of paying a speed/cost penalty on every prompt.

gotchas.problem: Claude starts coding before it fully understands the problem.
gotchas.solution: Use `/plan` or `--permission-mode plan` for anything multi-file, ambiguous, or architecture-heavy. Switch to implementation only after you like the plan.

gotchas.problem: You get okay-looking code that never got verified.
gotchas.solution: Always give a success command or artifact: tests, linter, screenshot, exact output, or repro steps. Ask Claude to run it before stopping.

gotchas.problem: Context quality degrades in long sessions and Claude starts forgetting earlier constraints.
gotchas.solution: Use `/context` to watch usage, `/compact` to trim history, and start a fresh or resumed session when the conversation becomes bloated.

gotchas.problem: Claude keeps asking for permission and the session becomes click-heavy.
gotchas.solution: Use `/permissions` or settings allowlists for safe commands. For read-only work use Plan Mode. Only use `--dangerously-skip-permissions` in a well-contained sandbox.

gotchas.problem: Your CLAUDE.md is huge, vague, or full of things Claude could infer anyway.
gotchas.solution: Keep it concise, specific, and focused on repo-specific instructions. If it grows large, split it with `.claude/rules/` or imports.

gotchas.problem: You describe files in words instead of pointing at them.
gotchas.solution: Use `@file` and `@directory` references. This is faster, more precise, and reduces wasteful exploration.
Use `-c`, `-r <session>`, or `/pr-comments <PR>` to reopen the existing session context instead of creating a brand-new chat.

gotchas.problem: Beginner prompts ask for 'fix it' with no reproduction steps or constraints.
gotchas.solution: Include the error, reproduce command, likely area, and what 'done' looks like. Claude performs much better with concrete scope.

gotchas.problem: Install works, but `claude` is not found.
gotchas.solution: Your install directory is probably missing from PATH. Verify `~/.local/bin` is on PATH on macOS/Linux, or `%USERPROFILE%\.local\bin` on Windows.
Use `claude --debug "hooks"` to trace hook events. Check `/hooks` in the TUI or run `claude doctor` to verify hook config is loading.

cliFlags.description: Run headless: Claude completes the task and exits instead of opening the interactive TUI.
cliFlags.useCase: Use in scripts, CI, one-shot analysis, and quick answers without starting a full session.

cliFlags.description: Minimal scripted mode: skips hook discovery, skills, plugins, MCP, auto-memory, and CLAUDE.md loading. Requires ANTHROPIC_API_KEY or apiKeyHelper. Use with -p for fast scripted calls.
cliFlags.useCase: CI automation and scripted pipelines where startup time matters and interactive features are not needed. Added v2.1.81.

cliFlags.description: Resume the most recent conversation in the current directory.
cliFlags.useCase: Best for daily work when you want to pick up where you left off without reloading context manually.

cliFlags.description: Resume a specific session by ID or name.
cliFlags.example: claude -r "auth-refactor" "Finish the migration and run tests"
cliFlags.useCase: Use when juggling multiple parallel tasks and you need the exact old thread, not just the latest one.

cliFlags.description: Start a session in a specific permission mode, including Plan Mode for read-only exploration.
cliFlags.useCase: Use `plan` for safe research before edits, especially in unfamiliar repos or risky changes.

cliFlags.description: Start Claude in an isolated git worktree so all changes happen on a clean branch without touching your working tree.
cliFlags.example: claude --worktree "Implement the feature and open a PR"
cliFlags.useCase: Safe experimentation and parallel feature work. Subagents can also use `isolation: "worktree"` in their config.

cliFlags.description: Choose the model for the session. Use model names or aliases like `sonnet`, `opus`, `haiku`.
cliFlags.useCase: Use stronger models for planning, architecture, or hard bugs; keep everyday work on faster defaults.

cliFlags.description: Adjust adaptive reasoning depth for the session: `low`, `medium`, `high` (persist across sessions), or `max` (current session only, requires Opus 4.6).
cliFlags.example: claude --effort high "Trace the real root cause of this flaky test"
cliFlags.useCase: Use when the problem is genuinely hard; don't spend extra latency/cost on routine edits.

cliFlags.description: Use a named preconfigured agent for the current session, overriding the default agent setting.
cliFlags.useCase: Activate a project-specific agent persona without having to specify it in settings each time.

cliFlags.description: Give Claude access to additional directories outside the main working directory.
cliFlags.useCase: Useful in monorepos, split repos, or when implementation depends on sibling projects.

cliFlags.description: Allow specific tools or command patterns to run without asking for approval.
cliFlags.useCase: Great for reducing permission fatigue while still avoiding full permission bypass.

cliFlags.description: Restrict which tools are available in the session (different from allowedTools which controls permissions).
cliFlags.useCase: Use to create tightly scoped sessions where Claude can only use specific tools.

cliFlags.description: Skip all permission prompts entirely. Use `--allow-dangerously-skip-permissions` to enable it as an option without activating immediately (composable with --permission-mode).
cliFlags.useCase: Only for tightly sandboxed or disposable environments where full autonomy is worth the risk.

cliFlags.description: Append custom text to the end of the default system prompt for the session.
cliFlags.example: claude --append-system-prompt "Always use TypeScript. Prefer functional style."
cliFlags.useCase: Add per-run rules without modifying CLAUDE.md or creating a new agent config.

cliFlags.description: Define custom subagents inline as JSON for the current session only.
cliFlags.example: claude --agents '{"reviewer":{"description":"Reviews code","prompt":"You are a code reviewer"}}'
cliFlags.useCase: Handy for temporary specialist agents in automation or experiments without creating files.

cliFlags.description: Set output format for headless (-p) mode: `text`, `json`, or `stream-json`.
cliFlags.useCase: Use `json` for machine-readable automation, `stream-json` for real-time processing in pipelines.

cliFlags.description: Limit how many agentic turns Claude can take in print mode.
cliFlags.useCase: Useful in CI or scripts where you want bounded runtime and predictable behavior.

cliFlags.description: Set a cost ceiling for API calls in print mode.
cliFlags.example: claude -p --max-budget-usd 1.50 "Review this patch for security bugs"
cliFlags.useCase: Use in automation to stop costs from drifting upward on open-ended tasks.

cliFlags.description: Enable Chrome browser integration for web automation, screenshots, and UI debugging.
cliFlags.useCase: Use for front-end debugging, visual verification, and end-to-end browser tasks.

slashCommands.description: Generate or improve a project CLAUDE.md based on the current repo. Set CLAUDE_CODE_NEW_INIT=true for an interactive flow that also sets up skills, hooks, and personal memory.
slashCommands.tip: Run this early in a new repo, then trim aggressively so only high-value instructions remain.

slashCommands.description: Enter plan mode directly from the prompt for read-only analysis and planning.
slashCommands.tip: Use before migrations, refactors, or any task that spans multiple files or uncertain requirements.

slashCommands.description: Compress the conversation to recover context while preserving the important thread.
slashCommands.example: /compact Keep only the debugging summary, failing test, and pending next steps.
slashCommands.tip: Give compact instructions; otherwise Claude may preserve the wrong parts of the conversation.

slashCommands.description: Visualize current context usage as a colored grid. Shows optimization suggestions for context-heavy tools, memory bloat, and capacity warnings.
slashCommands.tip: Check this before long sessions get sloppy; it's an early warning system for degraded performance.

slashCommands.description: Ask a quick side question without adding it to the conversation history or affecting the main task.
slashCommands.example: /btw What's the difference between useEffect and useLayoutEffect?
slashCommands.tip: Use this whenever you have a quick clarifying question mid-session that doesn't belong in the thread.

slashCommands.description: View and manage hook configurations for tool events (PreToolUse, PostToolUse, SubagentStart, Stop, etc.).
slashCommands.tip: Hooks are deterministic guardrails. Wire up formatters, linters, or safety checks here instead of relying on prompts.

slashCommands.description: Make the current local session available for remote control from claude.ai or the Claude mobile app.
slashCommands.tip: Use this when you start a session locally and want to hand off to mobile, or pick it up from another device.

slashCommands.description: Manage permission rules and allowlists for commands/tools.
slashCommands.tip: Allowlist safe repeat commands like test/lint/git read operations to avoid approval fatigue.

slashCommands.description: Create, inspect, and manage custom subagents.
slashCommands.tip: Make a read-only reviewer or debugger first; they pay off quickly and keep your main context clean.

slashCommands.description: Manage Claude Code plugins: install, uninstall, list, and reload.
slashCommands.tip: Plugins can ship hooks, skills, MCP servers, and agents together as a unit.

slashCommands.description: Fetch and display comments from a GitHub pull request. Auto-detects the PR for the current branch, or pass a PR URL or number. Requires the `gh` CLI.
slashCommands.tip: Follow up immediately with 'Address all unresolved review comments' to action them in one flow.

slashCommands.description: Create a branch (fork) of the current conversation at this point. Alias: /fork.
slashCommands.tip: Use when you want to explore an alternative approach without losing your current thread.

slashCommands.description: Switch models during a session. For supported models, use left/right arrows to adjust effort level inline.
slashCommands.tip: Switch up only for genuinely hard reasoning tasks; keep default or Sonnet for routine coding.

slashCommands.description: Change adaptive reasoning depth. `low`/`medium`/`high` persist across sessions; `max` is current session only (Opus 4.6). `auto` resets to model default.
slashCommands.tip: Often a better move than switching models when the task just needs deeper thought.

slashCommands.description: Manage CLAUDE.md memory files and auto-memory behavior. View, edit, or disable auto-memory entries.
slashCommands.tip: If Claude repeats the same mistakes across sessions, fix your memory instead of re-explaining manually every time.

slashCommands.description: Open an interactive diff viewer for uncommitted changes and Claude's per-turn edits. Use arrows to browse files and turns.
slashCommands.tip: Use this before commit/PR creation so you catch broad or unintended edits early.

slashCommands.description: Show token usage statistics for the session.
slashCommands.tip: If cost spikes, it usually means context sprawl, over-broad exploration, or using a stronger model than the task needs.

slashCommands.description: Start fresh and clear conversation history. Aliases: /reset, /new.
slashCommands.tip: For a truly messy session, sometimes a clean restart beats trying to salvage bloated context.

slashCommands.description: Run diagnostics on Claude Code installation and settings.
slashCommands.tip: This is the first thing to try before spelunking through PATH, auth, or config issues by hand.

config.description: Default model or alias for Claude Code sessions.
config.recommended: Use `sonnet` for daily work, or `opus` if you regularly do heavy planning before coding.

config.description: Persistent adaptive reasoning level. `low`/`medium`/`high` persist. `max` is session-only on Opus 4.6.
config.recommended: Set to `medium` for general use; bump to `high` via /effort only when you routinely do complex debugging or architecture.

config.description: Default permission mode for new sessions.
config.recommended: Set to `plan` if you want Claude to explore safely by default before making changes.

config.description: Allowlist of safe commands/tools that can run without prompting.
config.recommended: Add common read/test/lint/git commands you trust, like `Bash(npm test *)`, `Bash(git diff *)`, and `Read`.

config.description: Denylist of risky paths or commands even if Claude might request them.
config.recommended: Block secrets by default, e.g. `.env`, `.env.*`, and sensitive directories.

config.description: Whether Claude includes built-in commit/PR workflow instructions.
config.recommended: Keep `true` unless your team already has custom git workflow skills or strict alternate conventions.

config.description: Whether `@` file suggestions should respect `.gitignore` patterns.
config.recommended: Leave it `true` unless you intentionally need ignored/generated files in the picker.

config.description: How long inactive sessions persist before cleanup.
config.recommended: Keep default unless you want shorter retention for privacy or disk hygiene.

config.description: Custom status line configuration for showing context, costs, rate limits, or git state. As of v2.1.80, supports rate_limits field with used_percentage and resets_at.
config.recommended: Set one up if you use Claude heavily; seeing context pressure in real time helps you compact before performance drops.
Only change this if you have a deliberate storage/workstation policy; otherwise leave it alone.

proTips: The single best prompt upgrade is adding a verification target: test command, linter, screenshot, or exact expected output.
Use Plan Mode for uncertainty, not for everything. If you can describe the exact diff in one sentence, just ask Claude to do it directly.
Prefer `@file` references over repo-wide exploration when you already know the area involved.
When a session starts drifting, check `/context` and compact before quality drops noticeably.
A short, repo-specific CLAUDE.md beats a giant handbook. Include only what Claude cannot infer from code.
If you repeat the same instruction every day, move it into CLAUDE.md, a `.claude/rules/` file, a hook, or a subagent.
Use `/diff` before asking for a commit or PR; it's the fastest way to catch accidental broad edits.
`claude -c` is one of the most useful daily commands. Resuming context is often better than starting over.
Headless mode becomes much more powerful when paired with `--output-format json`, `--max-turns`, and `--max-budget-usd`.
Subagents are not just for teams. A personal debugger or code-reviewer agent can keep your main conversation far cleaner.
Use `/btw` for quick side questions mid-session — it answers without adding noise to the main conversation thread.
Hooks fire deterministically at lifecycle points (PreToolUse, PostToolUse, Stop, etc.). Use them for formatters and safety checks instead of relying on prompts.
Use `--bare` with `-p` in scripts and CI — it skips hook/skill/plugin discovery for faster startup when you don't need interactive features.
/remote-control lets you hand off a local session to claude.ai or your phone mid-task without losing context.
If Claude is repeatedly violating a rule, the problem is often instruction quality or context overload, not model capability.
Use stronger models or higher effort selectively. The fastest workflow is usually Sonnet for execution plus better prompts, not always Opus.
